titleInfo.title: Two Sources of Chinese Buddhist Biographies: Stupa Inscriptions and Miracle Stories

mods.abstract: 148–181 : on Liu Sahe/Huida’s biographies and miracle stories. The earliest datable material is the miracle story about Huida in the Mingxiang ji. Huijiao seems to have been aware of this story and presented a summary of it in his biographies. Hui-chiao’s biography centering around stories about Asoka stupas and images, however, is primarily based on other sources. Daoxuan’s biography mentions both the miracle story in Mingxiang ji, similar but not identical, and the Gaoseng zhuan summary of that story, and Huijiao’s earlier biography. The main part of Daoxuan’s Huida biography is based on materials from other sources. The story of Liu Sahe/Huida was widely known in connection with the legend of Asoka stupas. See, for exampe, the Liang shu. It parallels certains themes in the accounts of Mingxiang ji and Gaoseng zhuan biography. Miracle stories associated with Asoka stupas and Buddha images included in Huida’s biography appear in Daoxuan’s Ji shenzhou sanbao gantong lu (664). The Mingxiang ji version says that Huida lived in the capital city at the end of the T’ai-yuan period (376–396). 169: The treatment of the four miracle stories in the Kao-seng chuan biography of Hui-ta in these two later sources shows that at least by the seventh century Liu Sa-he’s name was associated only with the Asoka stupa stories, and that the Asoka image stories circulated without any reference to this monk.

titleInfo.title: Monks and Magicians: Religious Biographies in Asia
